task_context.description: This is a comparative electromagnetic calorimeter geometry evaluation task that involves simulating three distinct detector topologies using Geant4 to assess their performance characteristics. The task requires building and testing three different calorimeter geometries (planar sampling, projective tower, and accordion-folded) with identical materials and layer specifications, then systematically measuring their energy resolution, response uniformity, shower containment, and linearity across multiple electron beam energies. The evaluation focuses on understanding how geometric design affects detector performance, particularly examining dead material effects and spatial response variations within each topology.
task_context.goal: Determine which electromagnetic calorimeter geometry (box, projective tower, or accordion) provides the best combination of energy resolution and response uniformity while assessing how dead material between detector elements affects each geometry differently
constraints: Same materials for all geometries (lead absorber + plastic scintillator)
Total depth must be approximately 20 radiation lengths
Energy sweep limited to 1, 5, and 20 GeV for tractable simulation
Must use electrons (e-) in Geant4 particle gun

observations.content: Task classified with 98% confidence

This workflow systematically evaluates three electromagnetic calorimeter geometries by generating each configuration, running simulations at multiple energies and positions, analyzing performance metrics, and comparing results to determine the optimal design for energy resolution and response uniformity

{'report': '# Electromagnetic Calorimeter Geometry Comparison Report

## Executive Summary

This report presents the results of a comparative evaluation of three electromagnetic calorimeter geometries (box, projective tower, and accordion) using Geant4 simulations. The study aimed to determine which geometry provides the best combination of energy resolution and response uniformity while assessing dead material effects.

## Methodology

### Geometries Evaluated
Three distinct calorimete
